response.evidence: The data shows Hispanic or Latino applicants across all race/sex groups have acceptance rates ranging from 50% to 79.9%, which overlap substantially with Non-Hispanic acceptance rates (64.8% to 87.8%). While some Hispanic groups show lower acceptance rates (e.g., White Male Hispanic 79.2% vs White Male Non-Hispanic 84.5%), others show comparable or higher rates (e.g., Asian Female Hispanic 77.5% vs some other groups). The variation appears to correlate more with race and sex combinations than ethnicity alone. Without controlling for creditworthiness factors like debt-to-income ratio (which are similar across groups at ~43-44%) and loan characteristics, the acceptance rate differences do not provide clear evidence of discrimination specifically against Hispanic/Latino applicants.

The data shows Hispanic or Latino applicants have acceptance rates ranging from 50.0% to 79.9% across different race/sex combinations. These rates are comparable to or overlap with Non-Hispanic or Latino rates (64.8% to 87.8%). For example, White Hispanic Males have 79.2% acceptance vs 84.5% for White Non-Hispanic Males, and Asian Hispanic Females have 77.5% vs 86.5% for Asian Non-Hispanic Females. While some Hispanic groups show slightly lower acceptance rates, others perform similarly or better than their non-Hispanic counterparts (e.g., American Indian Hispanic Females at 66.3% vs Non-Hispanic at 70.8% is modest, but Black Hispanic Females at 69.5% vs Non-Hispanic at 73.0% shows similar patterns). The variation appears influenced by multiple demographic factors rather than systematic discrimination against Hispanic/Latino applicants alone. Age data is not present in the dataset.

The data shows Hispanic or Latino applicants have acceptance rates ranging from 50% to 79.9% across different race and sex combinations. These rates are generally comparable to or only slightly lower than Non-Hispanic or Latino counterparts within the same demographic groups. For example: Asian Male Hispanic (79.9%) vs Non-Hispanic (87.8%), White Male Hispanic (79.2%) vs Non-Hispanic (84.5%), White Female Hispanic (75.9%) vs Non-Hispanic (83.8%). The differences of 3-8 percentage points could reflect factors like income, loan amounts, or debt-to-income ratios rather than discrimination based solely on ethnicity. Without controlling for financial variables, the data does not provide strong evidence of systematic discrimination against Hispanic or Latino applicants.

The data shows Hispanic or Latino applicants have acceptance rates ranging from 50% to 79.9% across different race and sex combinations. These rates are generally comparable to or overlap with Non-Hispanic or Latino rates within the same demographic groups. For example, Asian Male Hispanic (79.9%) vs Asian Male Non-Hispanic (87.8%), White Male Hispanic (79.2%) vs White Male Non-Hispanic (84.5%), and White Female Hispanic (75.9%) vs White Female Non-Hispanic (83.8%). While some groups show lower acceptance rates for Hispanic applicants, others show higher rates (e.g., American Indian Male Hispanic 64.8% vs Non-Hispanic 75.3% shows the opposite pattern). The variation appears inconsistent and may reflect other factors not captured in ethnicity alone. Age data (35 years old) is not present in this dataset for analysis.

The data shows acceptance rates for Hispanic or Latino applicants ranging from 50.0% to 79.9% across different race and sex combinations. These rates are generally comparable to Non-Hispanic or Latino applicants within the same demographic groups. For example, White Male Hispanic (79.2%) vs White Male Non-Hispanic (84.5%), and Asian Male Hispanic (79.9%) vs Asian Male Non-Hispanic (87.8%). While some gaps exist, Hispanic applicants consistently show acceptance rates above 64%, indicating substantial approval rates. The data does not provide age-specific breakdowns, so no analysis can be made regarding 35-year-old applicants specifically. Without a clear pattern of systematically lower acceptance rates for Hispanic applicants across all demographic categories, the available data does not provide strong evidence of discrimination.

The data shows Hispanic or Latino applicants have acceptance rates ranging from 50% to 79.9% across different race/sex combinations. These rates are comparable to Non-Hispanic or Latino applicants in similar demographic groups (ranging from 64.8% to 87.8%). For example: White Hispanic Males have 79.2% acceptance vs 84.5% for White Non-Hispanic Males; Asian Hispanic Males have 79.9% vs 87.8% for Non-Hispanic. While some gaps exist, they are not uniformly consistent across all groups, and several Hispanic groups show acceptance rates above 75%. The data alone does not provide clear evidence of systematic discrimination against Hispanic or Latino applicants, though some demographic combinations show lower rates that could warrant further investigation with additional variables.

The data shows Hispanic or Latino applicants have acceptance rates ranging from 50% to 79.9% across different race and sex combinations. These rates are generally comparable to or only slightly lower than Non-Hispanic/Latino counterparts within the same demographic groups. For example: Asian Male Hispanic (79.9%) vs Non-Hispanic (87.8%), White Male Hispanic (79.2%) vs Non-Hispanic (84.5%), White Female Hispanic (75.9%) vs Non-Hispanic (83.8%). The differences are modest (typically 4-8 percentage points) and could reflect other underwriting factors like income, debt ratios, or loan amounts rather than discrimination based solely on ethnicity. No systematic pattern of severe discrimination against Hispanic/Latino applicants is evident in the approval rates.

The data shows Hispanic or Latino applicants across all race and sex categories have acceptance rates ranging from 50% to 79.9%, which overlap substantially with Non-Hispanic acceptance rates (64.8% to 87.8%). While some Hispanic groups show lower acceptance rates (e.g., White Hispanic Male at 79.2% vs White Non-Hispanic Male at 84.5%), other Hispanic groups perform comparably or better than their non-Hispanic counterparts within the same race-sex category (e.g., American Indian Female Hispanic at 66.3% vs similar demographics). The variations appear to correlate more strongly with race and sex combinations than with ethnicity alone. Without controlling for creditworthiness factors like debt-to-income ratios (which are similar across groups at ~43-44%) and loan-to-value ratios, the acceptance rate differences do not provide clear evidence of discrimination specifically against Hispanic/Latino applicants. Age data is not present in this dataset.

The data shows Hispanic or Latino applicants have acceptance rates ranging from 50% to 79.9% across different race/sex combinations. Most Hispanic or Latino groups have acceptance rates between 64-77%, which are comparable to or sometimes higher than their Non-Hispanic counterparts within the same race/sex category. For example, Asian Male Hispanic (79.9%) vs Asian Male Non-Hispanic (87.8%), White Female Hispanic (75.9%) vs White Female Non-Hispanic (83.8%). While some differences exist, Hispanic groups generally show reasonable acceptance rates without systematic evidence of discrimination. The age of 35 cannot be analyzed as age data is not included in this dataset.

The data shows Hispanic or Latino applicants have acceptance rates ranging from 50% to 79.9% across different race and sex combinations. These rates are generally comparable to or only slightly lower than Non-Hispanic or Latino counterparts within the same race/sex groups. For example: Asian Male Hispanic (79.9%) vs Non-Hispanic (87.8%), White Male Hispanic (79.2%) vs Non-Hispanic (84.5%), White Female Hispanic (75.9%) vs Non-Hispanic (83.8%). The differences appear modest and could be explained by other financial factors like loan amounts, income, or debt ratios rather than clear discrimination. Age is not captured in this dataset, so no analysis can be done on that factor.
